ocar.opportunity: Brain connectivity data from fMRI, DTI, and electrophysiology was increasingly being represented as graphs, but researchers lacked a unified, accessible toolbox for computing the full range of complex network metrics (clustering, path length, centrality, modularity) relevant to understanding brain organization.
ocar.challenge: Graph-theoretic measures of brain networks vary in their biological interpretation, computational requirements, and sensitivity to thresholding and parcellation choices. Researchers needed both accessible implementations and conceptual guidance on appropriate use and interpretation of these measures.
ocar.action: The authors developed the Brain Connectivity Toolbox (BCT), a comprehensive MATLAB (and later Python) toolbox implementing dozens of complex network measures for brain connectivity analysis. They provided detailed documentation of the biological interpretations of each measure and appropriate use cases.
ocar.resolution: The BCT became the most widely adopted toolbox for complex network analysis of brain connectivity data, used across modalities from fMRI to connectomics. It standardized the computation and reporting of network metrics in neuroimaging and connectomics research, enabling reproducible comparative analyses.
ocar.future_work: Extension to directed weighted networks more representative of connectome data, and development of statistical frameworks for comparing network metrics across groups, remain active areas.

plain_language_summary: To study how the brain is organized as a network, scientists needed standard mathematical tools to measure things like how clustered connections are, how efficiently information can travel between regions, and which nodes are most central. This paper created a comprehensive software toolbox providing these network analysis tools for brain data, becoming the standard in the field.

summaries.beginner: The brain can be thought of as a network where neurons or brain regions are nodes and connections between them are edges. This paper created a collection of software tools for analyzing the mathematical properties of these brain networks, such as how clustered they are or which nodes are most important.
summaries.intermediate: The Brain Connectivity Toolbox (BCT) provides standardized implementations of complex network measures — including clustering coefficient, characteristic path length, betweenness centrality, and modularity — along with interpretation guidelines for brain connectivity data. It has become the standard analysis platform for both functional connectivity (fMRI/EEG) and structural connectomics research.
summaries.advanced: BCT implements graph-theoretic measures applicable to binary, weighted, directed, and undirected networks, covering local and global efficiency, small-world metrics, rich-club organization, and community detection. For connectomics, BCT provides ready-to-use implementations of measures computed on synaptic-resolution connectome graphs, though caveats include sensitivity to parcellation and thresholding choices, the absence of statistical null model testing built into the toolbox, and the challenge of interpreting measures designed for sparse networks when applied to dense connectomes.

discussion_prompts: How do the network metrics computed by BCT change when applied to synaptic-resolution connectomes versus coarser mesoscale connectivity maps, and what are the implications?
What statistical frameworks should accompany BCT measures to properly assess the significance of observed network properties in connectomics data?
How should researchers choose between the many available network metrics in BCT when analyzing a new connectome dataset, and what guides that choice?
